summary_one_line: Input must NOT already contain a 'Content-Type' header literal, so the parser reaches the auto-insert false branch when the magic string is decoded.

hypotheses.what_input_feature: The input must carry the ASCII literal 'Content-Type:' (bytes 43 6f 6e 74 65 6e 74 2d 54 79 70 65 3a) positioned where the harness feeds it to Curl_checkheaders, so the request reaches HTTPREQ_POST processing and exercises the !Curl_checkheaders("Content-Type") gate at line 2615.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: The cmplog winner seed embeds the exact literal 'Content-Type:' contiguously at offsets 0x24-0x32 ('....GContent-Typ' then 'e:'), the same string constant compared inside Curl_checkheaders, letting the harness drive the POST path that reaches and evaluates the blocker.
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: The naive loser seeds contain no 'Content-Type' substring; their header-region bytes are short fragments like 'pOARAMETER' or '%op..----' with no occurrence of the 14-byte magic string, so the comparison inside Curl_checkheaders is never satisfied and they sit on the true side / never reach the gate as resolved.
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: cmplog intercepts the strcmp/memcmp comparing the header name against the 'Content-Type' constant and records both operands in the CmpLogObserver buffer; I2SRandReplace then substitutes the constant operand into the input at matching offsets, planting the literal that naive's blind havoc/token stack must hit by chance and does not.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocker is the false side of a Curl_checkheaders comparison against the literal 'Content-Type'.
cmplog resolves the branch in 8/10 trials while naive never resolves it (0/10), a decisive I2S pair.
The cmplog winner-resolving seed contains the literal ASCII 'Content-Typ' / 'e:' at offsets 0x24 onward.
The 'e:' continuation of the Content-Type literal appears at the start of the next 16-byte row of the winner seed.
The naive loser seeds carry unrelated header fragments such as 'pOARAMETER' with no Content-Type literal.
cmplog adds I2SRandReplace, which scans the input for one CMP operand and substitutes the other operand at those offsets.
evidence_trail.exact_quote:
scans the input for byte sequences matching one
operand, and substitutes the other operand at those offsets

evidence_trail.claim: cmplog intercepts string/memory comparisons and records both operands per execution.
naive's mutator stack has no I2S capability.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: any of the above. No I2S.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: Ran `python3 tools/db_query.py lineage --branch 152 --role W --fuzzer cmplog --trial 1 --seed 6d68b5da252422ab`. The chain walked all 50 recorded ancestor levels (max) and contained NO I2S-floor (mutation_op = -) rows; the tool reported 'No I2S-floor rows in this chain (all ancestors have a mutator list).' Floor is a lower bound, so I2S contribution may still exist in the leaked havoc bucket (TokenReplace/Bytes* finds that wrap I2S as a sub-mutator and tag the result with their own names). The byte-level evidence (exact 'Content-Type:' literal materialized only in the cmplog seed, absent in all naive seeds) independently supports the I2S substitution attribution despite the unverified floor.

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: A synthetic harness gating on an exact 'Content-Type' string comparison where naive resolves the false branch at a rate comparable to cmplog, showing the literal is reachable by blind havoc without CMP introspection.

weakest_evidence_point: The I2S-floor lineage signal was absent (all-havoc chain to the 50-level cap), so the I2SRandReplace attribution rests on the byte-level appearance of the exact literal rather than a confirmed dash-row ancestor.
